plainMeaning: We bow to Lord Pārśvanātha, whose very form is the sacred sound 'Hrīṃ', whose feet are worshipped by the guardian deities Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī, and who is a great soul possessing countless pure perfections.
recitation: hrīṃ rūpāya dharaṇīndra-padmāvaty-arcitāṅghraye, śuddhātiśaya-koṭibhiḥ sahitāya mahātmane.

wordByWord.gloss: the seed-syllable 'Hrīṃ' (kept as the Lord's mantric form; not translated)
wordByWord.etymology: bīja-mantra; the māyā-bīja hrīṃ, traditionally bound to Pārśva and his yakṣiṇī Padmāvatī. Preserved verbatim as mantra-text per the hymn's own rule.
wordByWord.notes: Reads with rūpāya as the bahuvrīhi hrīṃ-rūpa, 'whose form is hrīṃ.'

wordByWord.etymology: dative sg. of rūpa ('form, shape, embodiment'), from √rūp. Datival ending -āya marks the object of the salutation (namaḥ) carried over from verse 1.
wordByWord.notes: hrīṃ + rūpāya = hrīṃ-rūpāya, a bahuvrīhi: 'to him who has hrīṃ as his form.'

wordByWord.etymology: dharaṇī ('earth') + indra ('lord') = 'lord of the earth/nāgas'; here the first member of a dvandva joined with padmāvatī in the following compound.
wordByWord.notes: Spelt ધરણીંદ્ર in the source; variant ધરણેન્દ્ર elsewhere. Reads together with the next word: '(by) Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī.'

wordByWord.gloss: to him whose feet are worshipped by (Dharaṇendra and) Padmāvatī
wordByWord.etymology: sandhi of padmāvatī + arcita + aṅghri → dative sg. aṅghraye. arcita ('worshipped, honoured', past part. of √arc) qualifies aṅghri ('foot', a classical poetic term); padmāvatī (with dharaṇīndra) is the agent of worship.
wordByWord.notes: Resolves as dharaṇīndra-padmāvatī-arcita-aṅghraye: 'to him whose feet are honoured by Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī.' i/ī + a → ya (padmāvaty-arcita); a + a → ā (arcitāṅghri).

wordByWord.etymology: śuddha ('pure, untainted', from √śudh) + atiśaya ('supereminence, supernatural mark'); first member of an instrumental phrase with koṭibhiḥ.
wordByWord.notes: atiśaya here denotes the Tīrthaṅkara's wondrous excellences; 'pure' stresses their flawless, karma-free nature.

wordByWord.etymology: instrumental pl. of koṭi ('ten million, crore; a vast number'). The instrumental governs sahitāya: 'endowed with crores (of).'
wordByWord.notes: Hyperbolic plural conveying boundless number, not a literal count.

wordByWord.gloss: to him who is endowed with / accompanied by
wordByWord.etymology: dative sg. of sahita ('joined, accompanied, possessed of'), from sa- + √dhā / √hi. Takes the instrumental śuddhātiśaya-koṭibhiḥ as its complement.
wordByWord.notes: Another datival epithet under verse 1's namaḥ.

wordByWord.etymology: dative sg. of mahātman (mahā 'great' + ātman 'soul/self'); a bahuvrīhi, 'one whose soul is great.'
wordByWord.notes: Caps the chain of datives; the final object of the salutation.

literalTranslation: (Salutation) to him whose form is Hrīṃ, to him whose feet are worshipped by Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī, to the great-souled one endowed with crores of pure excellences (atiśayas).
idiomaticTranslation: Homage to Pārśva, whose very being is the radiant seed-sound Hrīṃ; at whose feet the serpent-lord Dharaṇendra and the goddess Padmāvatī bow in worship; the great-souled Lord adorned with ten million flawless marks of perfection.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Grammatically this verse is not a new sentence: its four dative epithets (rūpāya, …aṅghraye, sahitāya, mahātmane) all still hang on the namaḥ of the opening verse, so the reciter is uttering one continuous act of homage. What changes is the register. Verse 1 saluted Pārśva devotionally as 'god of gods' and 'bestower of all welfare'; verse 2 quietly shifts into the mantric key that will dominate the rest of the hymn by declaring hrīṃ-rūpāya — that the Lord's form simply *is* the seed-syllable Hrīṃ. This is the pivot of the whole stotra: the visible Jina and the audible bīja are identified, so that 'remembering the name' (the hymn's promise) becomes the same act as meditating on the syllable. The middle epithet frames that mantric form between the two guardian deities — Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī — whose feet-worship (arcitāṅghri) certifies Pārśva's supremacy: even the powerful nāga-king and yakṣiṇī are devotees, not equals. The closing pair, śuddhātiśaya-koṭibhiḥ sahitāya mahātmane, supplies the doctrinal ground for such worship: he is 'great-souled' because endowed with crores of *pure* excellences. The adjective śuddha is doing real work — it distinguishes a Tīrthaṅkara's atiśayas from mere magical power: they are the spontaneous overflow of a soul cleansed of karma, not contrivances. Thus in four compact epithets the verse moves from sound (hrīṃ) to devotion (the deities) to perfected being (mahātman), preparing the reciter to take up the actual bīja-mantras that follow.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Two Jain ideas anchor the verse. First, the doctrine of atiśaya: a Tīrthaṅkara manifests extraordinary excellences, but here they are qualified as śuddha — pure — underscoring that they arise from total karmic purity (the kevalin state), not from acquired siddhi; their being counted in koṭis is the idiom of infinitude, not arithmetic. Second, the identification of the Jina's rūpa with the bīja hrīṃ reflects the tāntric stratum of medieval Śvetāmbara devotion, in which nāma (name/sound) and sthāpanā (form/image) are treated as non-different supports for the same object of worship — a premise the meditation in the later verses depends on. The subordination of Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī as worshippers (not objects) keeps the hymn doctrinally orthodox: śāsana-devatās serve the path; liberation belongs to the vītarāga Jina alone.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, a practitioner does two things at once: visualizes Pārśva's image and hears that image resolve into the single syllable Hrīṃ held in the heart — a beginner can simply dwell on 'his form is Hrīṃ.' Picturing Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī bowing at his feet steadies the mind in a protective, reverent mood, and recalling his 'pure perfections' redirects the request from worldly power toward the flawless qualities worth emulating.

crossReferences.text: Opening verse of this hymn — 'oṃ namo deva-devāya … śrīmate pārśvanāthāya' (smaran-6/01), which supplies the namaḥ governing this verse's datives
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Colophon verse naming the same guardian pair — Jīrāpallī Pārśva 'served by the Pārśva-yakṣa and worshipped by Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī' (smaran-6/12)
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Atiśaya — definitions (incl. Jainism: the supernatural excellences of a Tīrthaṅkara)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of atiśaya and its specifically Jain sense (Tīrthaṅkara's supernatural excellences) for śuddhātiśaya.

sources.title: Aṅghri — Sanskrit-English definitions (Monier-Williams etc.)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming aṅghri = 'foot' (classical/poetic) for the compound padmāvaty-arcitāṅghraye.

sources.title: Mahātman — Sanskrit-English definitions
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of mahātman ('great-souled one') and its dative mahātmane.

sources.title: Parshvanatha (23rd Tīrthaṅkara) and his śāsana-devatās Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Dharaṇendra (nāga-king) and Padmāvatī (yakṣiṇī) as Pārśva's guardian deities who worship his feet.

notes: The verse has no finite verb; all four datives continue the salutation (namaḥ) of verse 1 — a verse-specific grammatical dependence, not a restatement of the sutra's arc. Sandhi resolution: padmāvaty-arcita (ī+a→ya), arcitāṅghri (a+a→ā), with dharaṇīndra-padmāvatī read as a dvandva agent of arcita. The source spells the deity ધરણીંદ્ર (dharaṇīndra); ધરણેન્દ્ર (dharaṇendra) is a common variant. Per the hymn's own rule the bīja hrīṃ is preserved verbatim, not translated. koṭi ('crore') is hyperbolic for boundless number. hrīṃ-rūpāya is read as a bahuvrīhi ('whose form is hrīṃ'); a looser reading 'to the beautiful one, by the form hrīṃ' is possible but less idiomatic in context.
